title: Preventing Token Over-Generation
concept: Setting custom stop sequences (like <eos>) to prevent the model from writing prose after finishing code.

exercises.prompt: What is one key objective during the SFT step in fine-tuning?

options.text: To improve model performance by minimizing loss
To increase the number of training tokens

exercises.explain: Minimizing loss helps the model to learn better and produce more accurate outputs.

cards.title: What Are Stop Sequences?
cards.body: Stop sequences are special markers that tell the model when to stop generating text. This can help prevent it from writing extra unwanted content.

cards.title: Definition of Stop Sequences
cards.body: Stop sequences, like <eos>, are specific tokens used in text generation to signal the end of a response or a completion.

cards.title: Stop Sequences as Traffic Lights
cards.body: Think of stop sequences like traffic lights for writing. Just as a red light tells cars to stop, a stop sequence tells the model to stop generating text.

cards.title: Example of Using <eos>
cards.body: If you set <eos> as a stop sequence, the model will halt its generation when it encounters this token, avoiding unnecessary text.

cards.title: Myth: More Tokens Are Always Better
cards.body: It's a common misconception that more generated text equals better output. Actually, stop sequences help keep the output relevant and concise.

cards.title: Comparison of Outputs With and Without Stop Sequences
cards.body: Using stop sequences can greatly enhance the clarity of generated text.

left.text: Function to add two numbers: function add(a, b) { return a + b; } The function simply takes two inputs and returns their sum. Remember, you can also implement error handling...

right.text: Function to add two numbers: function add(a, b) { return a + b; }<eos>

exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of a stop sequence in text generation?

options.text: To prevent unwanted text after completion
To calculate the number of tokens

exercises.explain: Stop sequences are used specifically to avoid extra text after a completion, ensuring clarity and precision.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Setting a stop sequence can help a model produce longer outputs.
exercises.explain: It is false; stop sequences are meant to cut off generation and prevent unnecessary long outputs.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: Stop sequences like <eos> help the model know when to __________.
exercises.sentence: Stop sequences like <eos> help the model know when to stop generating text.
exercises.explain: The exact phrase 'stop generating text' is what defines the purpose of stop sequences.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes the function of a stop sequence?

options.text: It helps the model remember previous inputs.
It signals the model to end generation at a specific point.
It increases the model's creativity in output.

exercises.explain: The correct description of a stop sequence is that it tells the model when to stop generating, allowing for more controlled outputs.

exercises.prompt: Why is it important to use stop sequences like <eos> in code generation?

options.text: To improve the length of the output
To ensure the output does not include irrelevant information
To allow the model to generate more examples

exercises.explain: Using stop sequences ensures that the output remains focused and doesn't include unnecessary details, resulting in more effective code generation.
